content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 8017 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 17, 2000


Kathy Howard
City Councilmember, City of West Covina
1428 East Merced
West Covina, California 91791

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-151

Dear Councilmember Howard:

This letter is in response to your request for advice about the conflict-of-interest laws in
the Political Reform Act (sometimes called the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Under the Political Reform Act, may you participate in the decision to purchase property
that is currently being leased by a Republican central committee if you are currently an unpaid
elected member of that committee?

CONCLUSION

Yes. You do not have a conflict of interest in that decision because you do not have an
economic interest in the Republican central committee for purposes of the Act.

FACTS

You are an elected member of the 60ᵗʰ Assembly District Republican Central Committee
("committee"), a political, nonprofit organization. The committee does not provide you with any
compensation, gifts or reimbursement. The committee leases office space in a strip mall from
the Scofield Family Trust. The City Council of West Covina, sitting as the West Covina

___________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-00-151
Page 2

Redevelopment Agency, may negotiate with the trust to acquire the property. Such an
acquisition could result in: (1) termination of the lease and possibly increased rental costs for
new space; (2) relocation payments from the trust or redevelopment agency to the city; and
(3) assignment by the trust of the remaining lease term to the redevelopment agency.

ANALYSIS

Conflict-of-Interest Prohibition

Under the Political Reform Act, a public official may not make, participate in making, or
in any way attempt to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official knows, or has reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. (Section
87100.) The Commission has developed an eight-step approach for determining whether an
individual has a disqualifying financial interest in a decision. (Regulation 18700(b).)

1. Public Official

The conflict-of-interest prohibition only applies to public officials. (Section 87100.) As
a member of the city council, you are a public official as defined by the Act. (Section 82048.)

2. Conduct Covered

The prohibition covers specific conduct: making, participating in making, or attempting
to use one's official position to influence a governmental decision. (Section 87100.) These
terms are defined in Regulations 18702-18702.4. By voting and deliberating on the purchase of
property by the redevelopment agency, you will be engaging in conduct regulated by the Act.
(Regulation 18702.1.)

3. Economic Interests

A public official has a disqualifying financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, on the official, or on the following enumerated economic interests:

1. Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$1,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)
2. Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $1,000 or
more. (Section 87103(b).)
3. Any source of income of $250 or more provided to the official within 12 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(c).)
4. Any business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

File No. A-00-151
Page 3

5. Any donor of gifts worth $300 or more provided to the official within 12 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(e).)

A decision will have a financial effect "on the official," within the meaning of Section
87103, if the decision will increase or decrease the official's personal expenses, income, assets,
or liabilities, or those of his or her immediate family. (Regulation 18703.5.) This is known as
the "personal financial effect" rule. Under your facts, it does not appear that the city's decision
to purchase the trust property will have any affect on your personal expenses, income, assets or
liabilities. Therefore, the personal financial effect rule does not apply.

In addition, you have not described any other economic interest that will be affected by
the city's decision to purchase the trust property. You do mention that you are an elected
member of the Republican central committee, which leases the trust property. However, you do
not receive any income from the committee, and the committee is not a business entity as defined
in the Act. (See Section 82005.) Therefore, you do not have an economic interest in the
committee on the basis that is a source of income to you, or on the basis that you hold a
management position on the committee. (Section 87103(c), (e).)

Since you have not identified any economic interest that may give rise to a disqualifying
financial interest in the city's decision to purchase the trust property, you do not have a conflict
of interest in that decision. You have also asked about other conflict-of-interest laws outside the
Political Reform Act, such as the Community Redevelopment Law in the California Health and
Safety Code, and Government Code Section 1090. The Commission's jurisdiction is limited to
the Political Reform Act. Consequently, we cannot advise you regarding the other laws
mentioned in your request. (Section 83111.)

For additional guidance, I have enclosed a conflict-of-interest fact sheet. If you have
other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Julia Bilaver
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Under the Political Reform Act, may you participate in the decision to purchase property
that is currently being leased by a Republican central committee if you are currently an unpaid
elected member of that committee?

CONCLUSION: Yes. You do not have a conflict of interest in that decision because you do not have an
economic interest in the Republican central committee for purposes of the Act.
